nodes.name: Literature Review
nodes.summary: Parent category for review questions about synthetic student responses in education and automated scoring research.
search_aliases: synthetic student responses
synthetic learner responses
LLM generated student answers
scope_notes: Use child review questions as the default work unit.
Keep source extraction separate from synthesis claims.

nodes.name: Use in Automated Scorer Validation
nodes.summary: Whether synthetic student responses have been used to validate, benchmark, calibrate, or stress-test automated scoring systems.
search_aliases: automated essay scoring synthetic responses validation
AI scorer validation synthetic student answers
automated short answer scoring generated responses
scope_notes: Separate scorer validation use from scorer training or augmentation use.
Record whether correctness labels come from humans, models, generated targets, or adjudication.

nodes.name: Synthetic Response Generation Methods
nodes.summary: Methods used to generate synthetic student responses, including templates, LLM prompting, fine-tuning, error conditioning, and hybrid workflows.
search_aliases: generate synthetic student responses LLM
student answer generation prompt rubric
synthetic educational response generation
scope_notes: Capture the generation target, model, prompt structure, sampling strategy, and any filtering or revision loop.

nodes.name: Synthetic Response Quality Evaluation
nodes.summary: How studies evaluate whether synthetic responses are plausible, label-faithful, diverse, realistic, or useful for downstream scoring work.
search_aliases: evaluate synthetic student answers
synthetic response quality education LLM
human evaluation generated student responses
scope_notes: Distinguish quality judgments about realism from correctness judgments against a rubric.

nodes.name: Model and Generation Effects
nodes.summary: Evidence about how synthetic response quality varies across LLM families, model generations, decoding settings, and generation workflows.
search_aliases: LLM model comparison synthetic student responses
GPT generated student answers quality comparison
model generation effects education synthetic data
scope_notes: Avoid cross-model ranking claims unless the compared models, prompts, tasks, and evaluation metrics are aligned.

nodes.name: Prompt Engineering Effects
nodes.summary: Evidence about the role of prompt design, examples, rubric conditioning, target-label conditioning, and iterative prompting in synthetic response generation.
search_aliases: prompt engineering synthetic student responses
rubric conditioned student answer generation
few-shot generated student responses
scope_notes: Track prompt ingredients as method variables, not as universal best practices unless replicated evidence supports that claim.

nodes.name: Comparison With Real Student Responses
nodes.summary: How studies compare synthetic responses against real student responses using human labels, automated metrics, distributions, error patterns, or scorer agreement.
search_aliases: synthetic versus real student responses
generated student answers compared to real
LLM generated responses real student data comparison
scope_notes: Record comparator dataset details and whether real responses share the same items, grade band, domain, and rubric.

nodes.name: Synthetic Response Scoring Agreement
nodes.summary: How synthetic student response studies use human labels, AI scores, model-generated labels, rubric scores, or scorer agreement to evaluate, label, validate, or benchmark generated responses.
search_aliases: human AI scoring agreement synthetic student responses
automated scoring agreement generated student responses
rubric scoring agreement LLM generated student answers
human scored generated student essays
synthetic response label validation
example_entities: quadratic weighted kappa on generated essays
scope_notes: Only include human-AI or scorer-agreement evidence when it is used to evaluate, label, validate, or benchmark synthetic or generated student responses.
Exclude generic automated-scoring agreement studies and LLM-as-judge studies unless the scored response corpus includes synthetic student responses.
Keep agreement metrics tied to response origin, label source, rubric label space, and whether real responses are part of the comparator.

nodes.name: Acceptable Uses in Automated Scoring
nodes.summary: Where synthetic student responses are appropriate, limited, or inappropriate in automated scoring research, development, validation, calibration, benchmarking, stress testing, and deployment-readiness workflows.
search_aliases: acceptable uses synthetic student responses automated scoring
synthetic student responses automated scoring development validation
synthetic data automated scorer research development validation
LLM generated student answers scorer training validation boundaries
synthetic response challenge sets automated scoring
scope_notes: Focus on use-case boundaries rather than whether one scorer agrees with one label set.
Distinguish research exploration, scorer development, training augmentation, calibration, stress testing, validation, and deployment-readiness claims.
Record when synthetic responses are acceptable only as supporting or pre-pilot evidence and when real student responses or independently human-scored data remain required.
Exclude generic scorer-validation evidence unless it makes an explicit claim about acceptable or unacceptable synthetic-response use.

nodes.name: Assessment Stakes and Use Boundaries
nodes.summary: How acceptable uses of synthetic student responses vary across formative, in-term, summative, high-stakes, and low-stakes assessment contexts.
search_aliases: synthetic student responses assessment stakes
synthetic responses formative summative automated scoring
synthetic student responses high stakes assessment validation
generated student answers low stakes formative feedback
scope_notes: Classify assessment purpose separately from decision consequence.
Distinguish low-stakes formative support from high-stakes validation or deployment-readiness claims.
Treat synthetic responses as developmental or diagnostic evidence unless source-backed validation supports a stronger operational role.

nodes.name: Use to Supplement Sparse or Imbalanced Response Data
nodes.summary: How synthetic student responses are used to supplement sparse, imbalanced, unavailable, or incomplete student-response datasets in automated scoring and assessment research.
search_aliases: synthetic student responses sparse dataset augmentation
imbalanced automated scoring generated student answers
synthetic constructed response data augmentation
high scoring responses underrepresented automated scoring
scope_notes: Include studies where sparsity, imbalance, limited labeled data, or unavailable real responses motivates the synthetic response workflow.
Record the role synthetic data played in the research process, such as scorer training, augmentation, validation, benchmarking, calibration, stress testing, or rubric development.
Keep essay scoring, short-answer scoring, constructed response, and multimodal/modeling tasks distinguishable.
